text: An intraoperative ultrasound is used to locate a massive cyst, which is clearly visible on the imaging.
The surgeon performs an anterior gastrotomy in the pre-pyloric area, suturing the superior flap back with silk sutures to improve exposure and free a hand for further surgical maneuvers.
The surgeon begins to open the posterior wall, performing the procedure on both sides.
The surgeon encounters bleeding in the surgical field and takes control to manage the situation.
The surgeon waits to place a figure-eight stitch to control bleeding at the surgical site.
The surgeon attempts to grasp the bleeding site with surgical instruments, applies suction, and puts pressure on the area, but bleeding persists.

The surgeon applies pressure with a surgical seal to slow down the bleeding, then places a figure-eight suture using a 3-0 Prolene stitch to control the hemorrhage. The cyst is opened to allow a full-thickness bite for better visualization and management of the bleeding site.
The surgeon carefully enters the cyst wall, using a cautery device to manage bleeding while navigating through the tissue.
The surgeon opens the cyst wall further to decompress the cyst, revealing the internal structure and reducing pressure.
The surgeon carefully uses electrocautery to bovie around the cyst wall, taking time to ensure precise dissection and control of any potential bleeding.
The surgeon uses a V-lock stitch to suture the cyst wall to the posterior wall of the stomach, ensuring the pseudocyst remains open for complete drainage.
The surgeon takes suturing bites to control a small bleeder in the surgical field, aiming to stop the bleeding with the current or next stitch.

The surgeon carefully sutures the cyst wall to the posterior wall of the stomach, ensuring the anterior stomach wall mucosa remains free and does not get inadvertently included in the sutures, which could cause gastric outlet obstruction.
The surgeon places another stitch on the opposite side, enlarging the opening in the wall of the pseudocyst. A portion of the burnt wall is excised and sent for pathology.

The surgeon continues suturing the thick wall of the pseudocyst to the posterior wall of the stomach, ensuring the cyst remains open for drainage. The pseudocyst wall appears plastered against the stomach wall, facilitating the suturing process.
The surgeon inspects the sutured pseudocyst to ensure it is completely empty, then performs a necrosectomy by removing necrotic tissue from the pseudocyst cavity.
Upon inserting the scope, a significant amount of bile is observed flowing out, following the completion of the cholecystectomy.
The surgeon passes a wire under fluoroscopic guidance, ensuring the wire advances into the duodenum.
The surgeon passes a temporary French seven-centimeter double pigtail catheter into the surgical site.
Under fluoroscopic guidance, the surgeon ensures the stent is loaded correctly, with a distal radiopaque marker and another marker on the blue deployment device visible on the screen.
Under fluoroscopy, the surgeon locates the proximal radio marker and adjusts its position by pulling it back slightly to ensure proper alignment.
The surgeon adjusts the positioning under fluoroscopy and fires a clip onto the cystic duct.
